text: We performed a uniportal VATS right upper lobe sleeve lobectomy.
The incision is at the fifth intercostal space.
Once the lung hilum is well exposed, we start dissection with electrocautery, identifying the superior vein, the anterior trunk of the pulmonary artery, and the azagus vein.
We like to keep some traction over the hilum to facilitate dissection and probably decrease vascular accidents.
We carry the dissection around the hilum towards its posterior portion.
With electrocautery, we open the pleura as far as possible down beyond intermediate bronchus.
With this procedure, we have a better exposure of the posterior portion of the airway.
Then we start with the blunt dissection
to expose main right bronchus, upper low bronchus, and intermediate bronchus.
With this, the posterior dissection is complete.
Now we proceed to an anterior dissection.
With thoracoscopic instruments, we dissect the anterior trunk of the pulmonary artery, keeping always the hilum retracted, which allows a safer blunt dissection.
The artery is divided with Ethican stapler 45 mm vascular load.
Next, to have a better exposure of the upper lobe vein, we divide the minor fissure with the same stapler device.
Dissection of the vein is carried out in a parallel angle
so to prevent arterial accidents.
Once the vein is fully dissected it is encircled with a vessel loop and with the same stapler device the vessel is divided.
Now we dissect between the right upper lobe bronchus and intermedius bronchus so we can complete
the dissection of the major fissure.
Again, we use the same stapler device, however this time with a green load for better tissue compression.
We dissect the back wall of the airway with the help of electrocautery and blunt dissection.
It is important to release well
both margins.
Once both right main bronchus and intermediate bronchus are well exposed, both are encircled with an umbilical cord.
We like to divide first the main right bronchus
always verifying the margins and the symmetry of the stump.
Usually, we confirm margins with the bronchoscopy inside the right main bronchus.
In the same way, we divide the intermediate bronchus
also confirming good oncologic margins.
Once margins are negative, the inferior pulmonary ligament is released for tension-free anastomosis.
A traction-staying suture is placed for a tension-free approximation of both bronchial stumps.
Anastomosis
is started on the inferior part of the cartilaginous portion of the proximal and distal bronchus using a 4-0 PDS.
A running suture is performed anteriorly and posteriorly also with a 4-0 PDS.
During the anastomosis, we kept the bronchoscope inside the right main bronchus to confirm good alignment between the stumps.
Once the anastomosis is concluded, we are happy with the final result and we check for air leak with underwater insufflation.
We like to add a pleural flap around the anastomosis to prevent direct contact between the artery and the anastomosis.
Once air leak and hemostasis are verified, a 24 French chest tube is inserted through the same intercostal space, however through a tunnel incision.
The patient was discharged home by fourth postoperative day.
And this is the bronchoscopy three weeks after surgery.
